issue_title: Funkiness when "text=auto" is in .gitattributes
issue_body:
I think I found an interesting edge-case kind of thing here... not sure if it's a problem that Peru can solve for itself, or if it's a weird Git thing.

If a Git repo is set to normalize line-endings (`core.autocrlf` in Git's config.), Peru thinks thinks "Imported files have been modified" if line-endings differ between files in Peru's cache and the repo's working directory.

To reproduce:

 1. Have a Git repo with these in it:
    - a `peru.yaml` file with one or more modules have contents with different line-ending characters.
    - a `.gitattributes` file with [`* text=auto`](https://help.github.com/articles/dealing-with-line-endings/#per-repository-settings) and/or [a matching global git config](https://help.github.com/articles/dealing-with-line-endings/#global-settings-for-line-endings).
 1. Run `peru sync`.

Expected:

 * Peru does what Peru does, dependences are populated in the working directory, and there is much rejoicing.

Actual:

 * If `peru sync` had already been run, Peru warns that "Imports would overwrite preexisting files (use --force to write anyway):" and lists the files it thinks are different.
 * Running `peru sync --force` (or just `peru sync` on a clean tree) prints this traceback:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/peru", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/peru/main.py", line 308, in main
    async.run_task(command_fn(params))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/peru/async.py", line 25, in run_task
    return asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(coro)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.4.3_1/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.4/lib/python3.4/asyncio/base_events.py", line 316, in run_until_complete
    return future.result()
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.4.3_1/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.4/lib/python3.4/asyncio/futures.py", line 275, in result
    raise self._exception
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.4.3_1/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.4/lib/python3.4/asyncio/tasks.py", line 236, in _step
    result = coro.send(value)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/peru/main.py", line 88, in do_sync
    params.runtime, params.scope, params.imports, params.runtime.sync_dir)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/peru/imports.py", line 14, in checkout
    force=runtime.force)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/peru/cache.py", line 164, in export_tree
    self._git('checkout-index', '--all', work_tree=dest)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/peru/cache.py", line 75, in _git
    raise self.GitError(command, output, process.returncode)
peru.cache.GitError: git command "git --git-dir=<working-dir>.peru/cache/trees --work-tree=<working-dir> checkout-index --all" returned error code 128:
<filename1> already exists, no checkout
<filename2> already exists, no checkout
<filename3> already exists, no checkout
...
<filenameN> already exists, no checkout
```

Other bits:

 * All of this goes away and Peru runs normally when the `core.autocrlf` setting is completely unset.
 * This also happens with `peru reup`, both the safe-fail because of perceived changes in the working dir, and the traceback when running with `--force`.

I was able to confirm that it's line-ending shenanigans that's getting Peru's Git guts tripped up. Despite the errors in the traceback, it looks like Peru is still getting files written out to the file system correctly. I _think_. Using Git to track the imported files themselves, the only changes to these files that Git sees are line-endings; do a `git stash && git stash pop` and the working directory will be clean again. Or, if doing a `peru reup --force`, the list of changed files will drop from "lots and lots" to only `peru.yaml` and whatever few files have changed between the two `rev`s.

issue_title: Confusing time display format in pandas DataFrame
issue_body:
attn @yugangzhang

Tom reports that the timezones coming out of the databroker at CHX are not correct. I wrote this simple script to investigate. It prints the local time, runs a dummy scan (no real hardware involved) and shows the table with timestamps. I find that the times agree.

I get this output:

```
Current Time: 2016-05-25 21:03:32.966762-04:00
                              time       det  motor
1 2016-05-25 21:03:33.645711-04:00  0.606531    1.0
2 2016-05-25 21:03:33.703810-04:00  0.135335    2.0
3 2016-05-25 21:03:33.765744-04:00  0.011109    3.0
```

As I run this, it is 5:03 PM in New York. The timestamps show GMT (Greenwich Mean Time) with a `-04:00` that denotes the adjustment to local time, which is set to 'US/Eastern' in metadatastore's configuration.

I am guessing that the times are correct for you as well, but that the display is confusing -- what with the `-04:00` off to the side. It seems we should just display the local time instead, not GMT - 04:00.

``` python
from bluesky import RunEngine
from databroker import db
from metadatastore.commands import insert
from bluesky.plans import scan
from bluesky.examples import motor, det
import time
import pandas as pd


# Setup a RunEngine with metadatastore.
RE = RunEngine({})
RE.subscribe('all', insert)

# Print the local time, for comparison.
secs = time.time()  # seconds since 1 January 1970
localized_time = pd.to_datetime(secs, unit='s').tz_localize('US/Eastern')
print('Current Time:', localized_time)

# Show timestamps from a simple scan.
RE(scan([det], motor, 1, 3, 3))
print(db.get_table(db[-1]))
```

Corvince

The issue seems to be that the ondns handler is setup first on the udp listener socket and so it is given all UDP traffic, but doesn't correctly manage non-DNS traffic. My problem is that I'm not following what the handler is doing well enough to suggest a fix. 

Perhaps onaccept_udp() could look at the port and if it is port 53 call ondns() ?

Perhaps sshuttle needs to listen on a second port if DNS is enabled, e.g. port 12353 and the iptables rules need to direct traffic to the other port:

`>> iptables -t mangle -A sshuttle-t-12300 -j TPROXY --tproxy-mark 0x1/0x1 --dest <ip> -m udp -p udp --dport 53 --on-port 12353`

This way UDP and UDP/53 traffic is handled separately?

My apologies for not being able to give a better report than forwarding UDP doesn't work with --dns but does without --dns.

issue_title: [MacOS, Linux] NuGet writes the NuGet.Config in ~/.config folder, but dotnet restore reads from ~/.nuget
issue_body:
When using NuGet to add a private feed, `NuGet Sources Add` writes in `~/.config/NuGet/NuGet.Config` file. But when using `dotnet restore` the `~/.nuget/NuGet/NuGet.Config` file is used to read the `packageSources` and `packageSourceCredentials`. 

This issue forces users to manually copy and paste all necessary information instead of having that generated automatically by the NuGet CLI. 

NuGet repo issue: https://github.com/NuGet/Home/issues/4413

issue_title: Factor public key out of fetch.py
issue_body: Now that `tools/eff-pubkey.pem` has been merged into the tree (#2577), we should factor the key out of `letsencrypt-auto-source/pieces/fetch.py` and have it automatically be added when running `letsencrypt-auto-source/build.py`.

issue_title: Existing Certificate <Replace> or <Cancel> question should actually default to <Reinstall>
issue_body:
Currently if there is an existing cert for the names you requested with `run`, you get this message:

`You have an existing certificate that contains exactly the same domains you requested (ref: /etc/letsencrypt/renewal/isnot.org.conf) Do you want to renew and replace this certificate with a newly-issued one?`

These are the wrong options.  Prior to the renewal window, the default option should be **\<Reinstall\>**.  After the typical renewal window has opened, the **\<Replace\>** default should become **\<Renew\>**.

Also we should convince ourselves that it's actually a good idea to ask these questions at all; and ensure that we have CLI flags to control the answer.

issue_title: Document REQUESTS_MAX_RETRIES
issue_body:
The [`REQUESTS_MAX_RETRIES`](https://github.com/django-salesforce/django-salesforce/blob/3597d1dcb0f3fea377a1d32f9fc0e554a9dd5759/salesforce/backend/__init__.py#L31) setting is used to retry failed requests.

I saw an issue when creating new SObjects where a `ReadTimeoutError` occurred while connecting to the SalesForce API.

The SObject was created on SalesForce's side, but requests retried the request ([as expected](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/9e9d2c6521e37e1c4f35e04f95a910ad47e28958/requests/adapters.py#L88)), which resulted in a duplicate SObject being created in SalesForce.

I'd like to see `REQUESTS_MAX_RETRIES` documented, and perhaps differing behavior between retrying GET requests, but not retrying POST/PUT/DELETE requests automatically in the SalesForce database backend.

I've changed `REQUESTS_MAX_RETRIES` to `0` in all of my projects using `django-salesforce` (which is also the [default in requests](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/9e9d2c6521e37e1c4f35e04f95a910ad47e28958/requests/adapters.py#L47))

issue_title: Implement user management
issue_body:
There is currently no way to manage users from the Dispatch admin dashboard. We have been using the `python manage.py createsuperuser` command to manually add users from the command line, but this is tedious and not very user-friendly.

We would like to extend the current person management tools available at https://www.ubyssey.ca/admin/persons/ and add the following features:
- Create new users
- Update user permissions (admin, editor, contributor)
- Update user passwords
- Deactivate users

The `Person` model has a one-to-one relationship with the `User` model. Every person can be associated with at most one user, and every user has to be associated with exactly one person. The `Person` model stores the basic information about the person, and is also used to keep track of authorship on articles and photos. The information required for authentication is kept separate in the `User` model, which makes authentication more secure (passwords, emails stored in their own table) and also removes the need for every person to be a user.

I was thinking we could simply update the current person form to be similar to the profile page at https://www.ubyssey.ca/admin/profile/, with the following changes:
- If a person is not yet a user, display a single email field followed by "Invite user" button. If an admin enters an email address and submits the form, Dispatch will send an email with a link that the invitee can follow to activate their new account. Once they click the link, they will be taken to a form that asks them to set a password for their account. 
- If a person is already a user, admins will see a form that allows them to reset the user's password, update their email, or change their permission level. 
- If a person is already a user, admins will see a button that says "Deactivate user". Clicking this will delete the `User` model associated with the person.

issue_title: workaround for strange characters in vim
issue_body:
Just wanted to write a quick guide for vim users, who using/or want to use powerline, but they get strange characters. I know Lokaltog/powerline is deprecated, but still, many uses that.

1. Install Lokaltog's powerline: https://github.com/Lokaltog/vim-powerline
2. Install patched-fonts(https://github.com/powerline/fonts), and set the new fonts in your terminal, like: Ubuntu Mono derivative Powerline
2. Copy this to your .vimrc file:
```
set laststatus=2
let g:Powerline_symbols = 'fancy'
set termencoding=utf-8
set encoding=utf-8
let g:Powerline_dividers_override = ["\Ue0b0", "\Ue0b1", "\Ue0b2", "\Ue0b3"]
let g:Powerline_symbols_override = { 'BRANCH': "\Ue0a0", 'LINE': "\Ue0a1", 'RO': "\Ue0a2" }
```

And thank god, it's working!

![ehqesln](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/9632419/8311900/ba131dae-19d9-11e5-8886-6d1c393125f7.png)

issue_title: Make an interface below B2RawApi that can be stubbed for testing.
issue_body:
The B2RawApi layer and the post_json() function that go with it have enough interesting code in them that they need to be unit tested.  I think the way to do this is to define a super-simple interface that wraps the urllib calls, which can then be stubbed out for testing.

The interface can take (url, headers, data) and return (status, headers, data).  It should be clear about what it returns when the URL is malformed, when there is a broken pipe, when the connection fails, and when there is a timeout.

issue_title: Validate field Y based on field X value
issue_body:
Hey,

I want to have a validation of field Y based on field X.

I could potentially do it with `post_load` decorator but it seems it overrides all errors (e.g. the validation dict contains only the error from method decorated with `post_load` - it misses some basic info that for example a field is missing).
Is there a way to tell post_load method that its validation failure should also consist other validations?

Or is there a way of accessing another validated fields inside a validator method (the one decorated with `validates`)?

issue_title: The last_match function should return None, never "undefined"
issue_body: The change to support replacing the session manager broke the `last_match()` function so that it returns the string `"undefined"` when there is no last match rather than `None` as it should.

issue_title: Overwrite hyper params when resume training
issue_body: In resume training, user needs to provide model and optim state (--model and --state ) of a snapshot for BigDL to resume the training from. It's often the case that user needs to adjust hyper-parameters in optim state (e.g. learning rate schedule, weight decay) in the resumed training. But in current implementations (as in googlenet example), overwriting hyper params in state file is not convenient. Should allow command line configurations to overwrite the configs in state.

issue_title: slow performance when assigning to empty DataFrame in Location.get_airmass and ModelChain.prepare_inputs
issue_labels: [{'id': 286475252, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyODY0NzUyNTI=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pvlib/pvlib-python/labels/easy', 'name': 'easy', 'color': '006b75', 'default': False}, {'id': 961364188, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5NjEzNjQxODg=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pvlib/pvlib-python/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': '97f4ee', 'default': True}, {'id': 961364003, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5NjEzNjQwMDM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pvlib/pvlib-python/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '3bcc79', 'default': True}, {'id': 987081529, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5ODcwODE1Mjk=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/pvlib/pvlib-python/labels/performance', 'name': 'performance', 'color': 'e06411', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Creating an empty DataFrame (no data, no index) and then filling it key by key can cause performance issues in some situations. I believe the issue is due to the way that pandas computes joins on the index. This happens in two places in pvlib: ``Location.get_airmass`` and ``ModelChain.prepare_inputs``. (determined with ``grep -r 'pd.DataFrame()' pvlib``). 

``Location.get_airmass``: this is most relevant with shorter input lengths, especially if ``solar_position`` is not supplied. I discovered this bottleneck when profiling a loop that called ``ModelChain.run_model`` on daily weather data.

``ModelChain.prepare_inputs``: this only an issue there if the user does not supply any weather data, in which case clear sky calculations will be run and the results assigned to the empty DataFrame. Less likely that anyone is running into a significant performance issue here due to the additional calculations, including a linke turbidity lookup.

Here's the key part of ``Location.get_airmass`` using an input of 1440 times, followed by two alternative implementations:

```python
%%timeit
airmass_relative = pvlib.atmosphere.relativeairmass(solar_position['zenith'])
pressure = pvlib.atmosphere.alt2pres(altitude)
airmass_absolute = pvlib.atmosphere.absoluteairmass(airmass_relative, pressure)

airmass = pd.DataFrame()
airmass['airmass_relative'] = airmass_relative
airmass['airmass_absolute'] = airmass_absolute
23.9 ms ± 780 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10 loops each)
```

Alternative 1:

```python
%%timeit
airmass_relative = pvlib.atmosphere.relativeairmass(solar_position['zenith'])
pressure = pvlib.atmosphere.alt2pres(altitude)
airmass_absolute = pvlib.atmosphere.absoluteairmass(airmass_relative, pressure)

airmass = pd.DataFrame(index=solar_position.index)
airmass['airmass_relative'] = airmass_relative
airmass['airmass_absolute'] = airmass_absolute
1.69 ms ± 28.4 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)
```

Alternative 2:

```python
%%timeit
airmass_relative = pvlib.atmosphere.relativeairmass(solar_position['zenith'])
pressure = pvlib.atmosphere.alt2pres(altitude)
airmass_absolute = pvlib.atmosphere.absoluteairmass(airmass_relative, pressure)

airmass = pd.DataFrame({'airmass_relative': airmass_relative, 'airmass_absolute': airmass_absolute})
airmass = airmass[['airmass_relative', 'airmass_absolute']]  # adds 0.4 ms, but guarantees same output 
1.43 ms ± 40.1 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)
```

Either 1 or 2 could work for ``Location.get_airmass``. Only 1 would easily work for ``ModelChain.run_model``.

**Versions:**
 - ``pvlib.__version__``: '0.5.2+16.g58f95e0'
 - ``pandas.__version__``: '0.23.1'
 - python: 3.6.5

Approximately reproduced on python 3.5 and pandas 0.17.

line profiler:
<details>

```
   258         1          2.0      2.0      0.0          if solar_position is None:
   259                                                       solar_position = self.get_solarposition(times)
   260                                           
   261         1          3.0      3.0      0.0          if model in atmosphere.APPARENT_ZENITH_MODELS:
   262         1         44.0     44.0      0.1              zenith = solar_position['apparent_zenith']
   263                                                   elif model in atmosphere.TRUE_ZENITH_MODELS:
   264                                                       zenith = solar_position['zenith']
   265                                                   else:
   266                                                       raise ValueError('{} is not a valid airmass model'.format(model))
   267                                           
   268         1       1466.0   1466.0      3.6          airmass_relative = atmosphere.relativeairmass(zenith, model)
   269                                           
   270         1          9.0      9.0      0.0          pressure = atmosphere.alt2pres(self.altitude)
   271         1          1.0      1.0      0.0          airmass_absolute = atmosphere.absoluteairmass(airmass_relative,
   272         1        639.0    639.0      1.6                                                        pressure)
   273                                           
   274         1        638.0    638.0      1.6          airmass = pd.DataFrame()
   275         1      36061.0  36061.0     88.2          airmass['airmass_relative'] = airmass_relative
   276         1       2019.0   2019.0      4.9          airmass['airmass_absolute'] = airmass_absolute
   277                                           
   278         1          0.0      0.0      0.0          return airmass
```

</details>

issue_title: Z3 Optimizer Unsatisfiability with Real Constraints
issue_body:
I'm having an issue where the optimizer declares that a set of constraints is unsatisfied if a constraint contains a real value that isn't reducible to an integer.

Specifically, the following code will report 'unsat':

```
(declare-datatypes () ((PartID (P0) (P1))))

(declare-fun n3_p () PartID)
(declare-fun n2_p () PartID)
(declare-fun n1_p () PartID)
(declare-fun n0_p () PartID)

(declare-fun imbalance () Int)

(assert
  (let ((a!1 (+ 0
                (ite (= n0_p P0) 4 0)
                (ite (= n1_p P0) 2 0)
                (ite (= n2_p P0) 1 0)
                (ite (= n3_p P0) 1 0)))
        (a!2 (+ 0
                (ite (= n0_p P1) 4 0)
                (ite (= n1_p P1) 2 0)
                (ite (= n2_p P1) 1 0)
                (ite (= n3_p P1) 1 0))))
       (= imbalance (ite (> a!1 a!2) (- a!1 a!2) (- a!2 a!1)))))

(assert (<= (to_real imbalance) (/ 2.0 25.0)))
(minimize imbalance)
(check-sat)
```

I have found three independent modifications that cause Z3 to declare the system satisfiable and produce a valid model:

1. Commenting out `(minimize imbalance)` or
2. Commenting out `(assert (<= (to_real imbalance) (/ 2.0 25.0)))` or
3. Making the bound on `imbalance` reducible to an integer (e.g. `(/ 2.0 2.0)`)

If, in the original system, you replace `(minimize imbalance)` with `(minimize 0)` Z3 still reports `unsat`.

Am I using Z3 incorrectly, is there a subtle bug in my program, or is this an actual issue with Z3?

issue_title: Provide option to link ``z3`` against ``libz3``
issue_body:
This is a suggested enhancement (related to #279). It is likely that Linux distro packagers will want to have the z3 executable linked against the ``libz3`` shared library to reduce the size of the ``z3`` binary.

Neither the CMake nor the Python based build system can do this right now and I think it would be useful to look at what is blocking this and discuss whether or not it is worth addressing.

AFAICT these are the issues that prevent having the ``z3`` from linking against ``libz3`` directly.

* The ``api_dll`` (in ``libz3``), ``shell`` (not in ``libz3``) and ``test`` (not in ``libz3``) components all generate their own versions of ``gparams_register_modules.cpp``, ``install_tactic.cpp`` and ``mem_initializer.cpp``. In Python build system these are identical. In the CMake build system these aren't identical (due to order paths are searched) but this can be fixed. If ``z3`` was to link against ``libz3`` then the ``shell`` component shouldn't generate its own versions of these files to avoiding linking problems due to identical symbols being defined multiple times. Is there any reason why ``z3``, ``libz3`` and ``test-z3`` get their own versions of these files? To me it would be simpler if those files were just another component which ``libz3``, ``z3`` and ``test-z3`` all used.

* Symbol visibility. This is the biggest issue. Because most of ``libz3``'s symbols are hidden the ``z3`` sources (i.e. the ``shell`` component) can't link against ``libz3``. It is not as simple as removing ``-fvisibility=hidden`` because on Windows symbols are hidden by default. I don't really know how to go about fixing this.

On the usability front #279 raised the issue that on UNIX like platforms if ``z3`` did link against ``libz3`` that the user would have to set ``LD_LIBRARY_PATH`` or ``DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH`` manually for an ``z3`` to work. This is actually only an issue for the Python build system. The CMake build system will automatically set the ``rpath`` on the ``z3`` binaries so that the system linker automatically knows where to find ``libz3``. It is also very easy to set  ``LD_LIBRARY_PATH`` or ``DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH`` so I don't think this particular issue is a strong argument against supporting ``z3`` linking against ``libz3``.

Another argument might be the performance impact of having ``z3`` use ``libz3``. Then again if you really want to get rid of link time performance hits you should compile ``z3`` as a static binary so again this isn't a strong argument to not support an option to build ``z3`` against ``libz3``.

Any thoughts?

issue_title: Z3: dispatch solving process into multiple threads
reconsider app init when testing CLI commands
issue_body:
Currently, `testing.invoke_command()` sets the environment variable `USE_TEST_PROFILE` which triggers the use of, wait for it, the testing profile.  But, `.cli.CLILoader.create_app()` inits the app directly and doesn't use `Keg.test_prep()` which means the app instance is not cached and the `testing_run_start` signal might not get sent.

It seems like a refactoring of `Keg.init()` to support the `testing_prep()` logic might be called for and that logic could be triggered by a keyword arg or environment variable.  One caveat though is that when using `invoke_command()` args might be sent to the app that affect it's instantiation.  If they are different, you wouldn't want to return the cached app.

issue_title: API: Multiple outputs in Layer.get_output_for()
issue_body:
In the context of recurrent neural networks, we've found two use cases that would need layers to be able to return multiple outputs:

1. @skaae wanted to return the hidden state along with the output for recurrent layers (I don't exactly recall why, please help me out, but I remember that there was no alternative to returning multiple outputs from a single `get_output_for()` call)
2. When training on batches of sequences of different length, it's necessary to mask out items that are to be ignored (or give the sequence lengths in another way). Currently, #294 solves this by adding a `mask` keyword argument to `get_output_for()`, but this only works for networks that have a single input layer only, or that have the same sequence lengths for all inputs. For more flexibility, the mask should be paired with the inputs as they are propagated through the network. This would require both the `InputLayer` and recurrent layers to return multiple outputs in `get_output_for()` (or, instead of having `InputLayer` returning multiple outputs itself, have a `MergeLayer` subclass that returns all its inputs instead of actually merging them).

While we don't have to solve this now, we should figure out whether it's possible to add this functionality later on without breaking the API. If so, we can postpone it until after the first release, otherwise we probably shouldn't.

A simple solution would be allowing `get_output_for()` to return a list, or a tuple, or a dictionary, or an OrderedDict of outputs alternatively to returning a single theano expression (we should pick one). A dictionary or OrderedDict would allow outputs to be named, such that the mask could be called `mask`, for example. This might improve readability of layers dealing with multiple inputs. As far as I can see, this could be added later without breaking existing code.
In addition, `get_output_shape_for()` would also have to be changed to return multiple shapes.

If we do this, the next question is what to stack on top. Only allow `MergeLayer` because it gets multiple inputs and input shapes? Or allow `Layer.get_output_for()` to accept a list/tuple/dictionary as its single `input` argument? How should it handle multiple input shapes then? Should we designate one of the outputs/output shapes as primary, so an ordinary `DenseLayer` or something could be stacked on top, oblivious of the extra inputs?

Do we have any other use cases that can help in design decisions?

issue_title: LogSoftmax as a non-linearity
issue_body:
The Softmax non-linearity can lead to potential instabilities during training, LogSoftmax (i.e. log(Softmax(x)) ) seems to alleviate the issue. See #308 and [Theano/Theano#3162](https://github.com/Theano/Theano/issues/3162).

It might be useful to include LogSoftmax as a non-linearity and a corresponding Cross-entropy objective that assumes output probabilities are already in the log domain.

issue_title: Retry error
issue_body: I just had an unusual 500 error from SES with this response: ``` Receiver InternalFailure An internal failure has occurred. 2e7d4c8d-3746-11e5-a140-b919bcc9ecab ``` Normally, when an error occurs, my server has logic to queue a task to retry the message later. But, on each subsequent retry, I got this error: ``` File "/base/data/home/apps/s~f/1.386013588458176163/django_ses/mail.py", line 35, in _send_deferred backend.send_messages([message]) File "/base/data/home/apps/s~f/1.386013588458176163/django_ses/mail.py", line 203, in send_messages raw_message=unicode(dkim_sign(message.message().as_string()), 'utf-8') File "/base/data/home/apps/s~f/1.386013588458176163/django/core/mail/message.py", line 260, in message msg[name] = value File "/base/data/home/apps/s~fare/1.386013588458176163/django/core/mail/message.py", line 190, in __setitem__ name, val = forbid_multi_line_headers(name, val, self.encoding) File "/base/data/home/apps/s~fare-clock/1.386013588458176163/django/core/mail/message.py", line 86, in forbid_multi_line_headers raise BadHeaderError("Header values can't contain newlines (got %r for header %r)" % (val, name)) BadHeaderError: Header values can't contain newlines (got u'\n \n Receiver\n InternalFailure\n An internal failure has occurred.\n \n 2e7d4c8d-3746-11e5-a140-b919bcc9ecab\n\n' for header 'body') ``` What's strange is it looks like the message had a header containing the error response from the original attempt to send the message. I looked through the code, and noticed that the exception handler in send_messages() looks like this: ```python except SESConnection.ResponseError as err: # Store failure information so to post process it if required error_keys = ['status', 'reason', 'body', 'request_id', 'error_code', 'error_message'] for key in error_keys: message.extra_headers[key] = getattr(err, key, None) if not self.fail_silently: raise ``` So it's storing the error as a message header, and sending it in the subsequent retry. Is it wrong to retry a message, or is this a bug?

Thanks for all your hard work on this - angr is amazing!

issue_title: Solver that finds values satisfying expression equivalence or not
issue_body:
Is there any way in Angr or Claripy to do the following:

Given two symbolic expressions, for example:

    <BV32 r0_0_32 + r1_0_32 + 0x4>
    <BV32 r0_1_32 + r1_1_32 + 0x4>

Find a tuple that satisfies both expressions?
Here it can be:

    r0 = 0, r1 = 0

If it cannot, return False.

It should know that `r0_0_32` and `r0_1_32` are the same thing. (this can be done by rewriting AST, just replace all the value in the middle to 0)

issue_title: DK2.0 Unrequested RANGEFINDER and VIBRATION output on startup
issue_body:
The following code should pretty much connect then close

```
from droneapi import connect
from droneapi.lib import VehicleMode
from pymavlink import mavutil
import time

api = connect('127.0.0.1:14550')
# Get the connected vehicle (currently only one vehicle can be returned).
vehicle = api.get_vehicles()[0]

time.sleep(20)
print 'exit'
```

What it does instead is print:

```
Completed list of 511 params
Starting dronekit.
RANGEFINDER {distance : 0.0899999961257, voltage : 0.00900000054389}
VIBRATION {time_usec : 4360865000, vibration_x : 0.0, vibration_y : 0.0, vibration_z : 0.0, clipping_0 : 0, clipping_1 : 0, clipping_2 : 0}
RANGEFINDER {distance : 0.0899999961257, voltage : 0.00900000054389}
VIBRATION {time_usec : 4361105000, vibration_x : 0.0, vibration_y : 0.0, vibration_z : 0.0, clipping_0 : 0, clipping_1 : 0, clipping_2 : 0}
... 20 seconds of reptition
RANGEFINDER {distance : 0.0899999961257, voltage : 0.00900000054389}
VIBRATION {time_usec : 4378145000, vibration_x : 0.0, vibration_y : 0.0, vibration_z : 0.0, clipping_0 : 0, clipping_1 : 0, clipping_2 : 0}
RANGEFINDER {distance : 0.0899999961257, voltage : 0.00900000054389}exit

```
So there is some unwanted output of rangefinder and vibration. I would also say that these messages are not needed - the user should add "connected" or whatever as soon as they have a vehicle.
```
Completed list of 511 params
Starting dronekit.
```

issue_title: Excessive Delay when polling vehicle.attitude, etc.
issue_body:
I am working on a multi-agent fixed wing UAV formation project. The agents share information over UDP broadcast via a raspberry pi v3B. In the main control thread, I poll various attributes (attitude, position, channels, etc) and am using roll angle as a test case for debugging.

I have found (by profiling) that  the program spends the overwhelming majority of time waiting (to have a reasonable sampling time). This is expected.  I have found that as the other threads consume resources (not many resources,  but some), the reads, to, say, vehicle.attitude.roll produce values that are delayed by up to about 1.5 seconds. The resulting values are generally unique, but lag behind real-time by  that amount.

I dug into the code, and it looks like a queue is being used in mavlink.py to get messages from pymavlink to dronekit. I suspect this queue is not being emptied fast enough. If this is, in fact, a likely cause, what is the cleanest way manually process all messages in this queue to get real-time data into the vehicle object?

issue_title: Adding message observer can prevent arming and attribute listener updates
issue_body:
@tcr3dr My initial test code for #445 is below. This adds a listener on_attribute for 'location' and a listener on_message for 'GLOBAL_POSITION_INT'. 

The problem is that the on_message is not reporting any messages. Worse, if I enable it the vehicle will not arm (even though it reports ``is_armable``) and only gives ONE update of the location information. Removing the offending code means that the example takes off and reports location as normal. You can verify this for yourself using the code below - simply (un)comment out the code marked up as ``#### DISABLE BELOW CODE TO ALLOW ARMING``

The create_attribute.py still works, so probably I am doing something wrong here ... but even so, we need to know why because this is very odd.


```
from dronekit import connect
from dronekit.lib import VehicleMode, LocationGlobal
import time


#Set up option parsing to get connection string
import argparse  
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Print out vehicle state information. Connects to SITL on local PC by default.')
parser.add_argument('--connect', default='127.0.0.1:14550',
                   help="vehicle connection target. Default '127.0.0.1:14550'")
args = parser.parse_args()

# Connect to the Vehicle
print 'Connecting to vehicle on: %s' % args.connect
vehicle = connect(args.connect, wait_ready=True)
print "Got connected baby"

@vehicle.on_attribute('location')   
def listener(self, attr_name, value):
    # `attr_name` is the observed attribute (used if callback is used for multiple attributes)
    # `attr_name` - the observed attribute (used if callback is used for multiple attributes)
    # `value` is the updated attribute value.
    print "ATTR: %s changed to: %s" % (attr_name, value)
    print " GlobalF: %s" % self.location.global_frame
    print " LocalF: %s" % self.location.local_frame
    
  
#### DISABLE BELOW CODE TO ALLOW ARMING

# Create a message listener using the decorator.   
@vehicle.on_message('GLOBAL_POSITION_INT')
def listener(self, name, message):
    print "gpi alt: %s" % message.alt/1000
    print "gpi relative_alt: %s" % message.relative_alt/1000  
    
#### DISABLE ABOVE CODE TO ALLOW ARMING

# Create an attribute listener using the decorator.  
# If this changes but we don't get on_message notifications we know there is a problem
@vehicle.on_attribute('location')   
def listener(self, attr_name, value):
    # `attr_name` is the observed attribute (used if callback is used for multiple attributes)
    # `attr_name` - the observed attribute (used if callback is used for multiple attributes)
    # `value` is the updated attribute value.
    print " ATTR: %s changed to: %s" % (attr_name, value)
    



def arm_and_takeoff(aTargetAltitude):
    """
    Arms vehicle and fly to aTargetAltitude.
    """

    print "Basic pre-arm checks"
    # Don't let the user try to arm until autopilot is ready
    while not vehicle.is_armable:
        print " Waiting for vehicle to initialise..."
        print " GPS: %s" % vehicle.gps_0
        print " Is Armable?: %s" % vehicle.is_armable
        print " System status: %s" % vehicle.system_status.state
        print " Mode: %s" % vehicle.mode.name    # settable
        time.sleep(1)

        
    print "Arming motors"
    # Copter should arm in GUIDED mode
    vehicle.mode    = VehicleMode("GUIDED")
    vehicle.armed   = True    

    count_to_arm=0
    while not vehicle.armed:
        count_to_arm+=1    
        print " Waiting for arming...: %s" % count_to_arm
        print " GPS: %s" % vehicle.gps_0
        print " Is Armable?: %s" % vehicle.is_armable
        print " System status: %s" % vehicle.system_status.state
        print " Mode: %s" % vehicle.mode.name    # settable
        print " Armed: %s" % vehicle.armed
        vehicle.armed   = True   
        time.sleep(1)

    print "Taking off!"
    vehicle.commands.takeoff(aTargetAltitude) # Take off to target altitude

    # Wait until the vehicle reaches a safe height before processing the goto (otherwise the command 
    #  after Vehicle.commands.takeoff will execute immediately).
    while True:
        print " Altitude: ", vehicle.location.global_frame.alt      
        if vehicle.location.global_frame.alt>=aTargetAltitude*0.95: #Trigger just below target alt.
            print "Reached target altitude"
            break
        time.sleep(1)

arm_and_takeoff(20)


print "Going to first point..."
point1 = LocationGlobal(-35.361354, 149.165218, 20, is_relative=True)
vehicle.commands.goto(point1)

# sleep so we can see the change in map
time.sleep(30)

print "Going to second point..."
point2 = LocationGlobal(-35.363244, 149.168801, 20, is_relative=True)
vehicle.commands.goto(point2)

# sleep so we can see the change in map
time.sleep(30)

print "Returning to Launch"
vehicle.mode    = VehicleMode("RTL")

#Close vehicle object before exiting script
print "Close vehicle object"
vehicle.close()

```

issue_title: Timing resistance fix
issue_body:
Check out the [itsdangerous](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/itsdangerous/blob/master/itsdangerous.py#L77) timing resistant compare function, which does not have the equality short-circuit:

```python
def constant_time_compare(val1, val2):
    """Returns True if the two strings are equal, False otherwise.
    The time taken is independent of the number of characters that match.  Do
    not use this function for anything else than comparision with known
    length targets.
    This is should be implemented in C in order to get it completely right.
    """
    if _builtin_constant_time_compare is not None:
        return _builtin_constant_time_compare(val1, val2)
    len_eq = len(val1) == len(val2)
    if len_eq:
        result = 0
        left = val1
    else:
        result = 1
        left = val2
    for x, y in izip(bytearray(left), bytearray(val2)):
        result |= x ^ y
    return result == 0
```

issue_title: TypeError: comparing strings with non-ASCII characters is not supported
issue_body:
at https://github.com/pyotp/pyotp/blob/8e490934a6d9ef27fdbbb7ec94c7ee9cf83cba9d/src/pyotp/utils.py#L69.

`hmac.compare_digest` does not support strings which contains non-ASCII characters. User typed codes would typically be ASCII, but sometimes with non-ASCII.

In particular, Japanese people often use FULLWIDTH digits (`\uff10`[０]  - `\uff19`[９]) instead of ASCII digits.

So, to prevent above exception, pyotp should normalize codes based on Unicode before pass to `hmac.compare_digest` or simply ignore `TypeError`.

issue_title: Preset for dense SLAM
issue_body:
I'm trying to use a D415 for a dense SLAM system. (For example KinnectFusion, or ElasticFusion, etc). These SLAM systems were designed and tested with a Primesense/Kinnect type of camera (structured light).  When I test these SLAM systems with a D415 I get very poor performance in comparison to Primesense.  I seem to get very sparse depth maps and if I use the HighDensity Preset I get too much noise. 

After playing for a couple of days with the presets found [here](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/wiki/D400-Series-Visual-Presets) and  the other settings that I can see in the realsense-viewer tool I improved a bit the depth map adjusting the following settings:
Starting from Default preset.
Disabling autoexposure: seems to make everything a bit less noisy.
Depth Control/DS second Peak th: high seems to make the scene less noisy at short ranges.
SLO Penalty Control/K2 penalty:  high value seems to make scene a bit denser.
Depth clamp: 800-6000 

However, it's still more sparse and noisy that with a primesense depth map, and the SLAM system doesn't work so well, the maps are substantially more sparse. 

I wonder if you could produce a preset for the D415 optimised for dense SLAM.

issue_title: snake_case field name not allowed
issue_body:
```python
import graphene


class Character(graphene.ObjectType):
    appears_in = graphene.List(graphene.String)

    def resolve_appears_in(self, info):
        return ["1", "2"]


class RootQuery(graphene.ObjectType):
    a = graphene.String(name=graphene.String(default_value="stranger"), y=graphene.String(default_value="xxx"))
    b = graphene.Field(lambda: Character)

        return 'Hello ' + name + y

    def resolve_b(self, info):
        return 123


schema = graphene.Schema(RootQuery)

result = schema.execute('{b {appears_in}}')
print {

}
```

Is this a bug or I can config it?

issue_title: Calculated/computed Fields resolve ordert
issue_body:
I'm working through providing a graphql source to an energy monitoring application. Total load for a premises is essentially the sum of all of the measured loads on each circuit.

The following is a trivial piece of the work where there are two measured load fields  and one total_load field

```
class Heating(graphene.ObjectType):
    total_load = graphene.Float()
    water_heater = graphene.Field(lambda: SensorReading,
                                  resolver=get_measurement,
                                  args={'id': graphene.String('Water Heater')})
    space_heater = graphene.Field(lambda: SensorReading,
                                      resolver=get_measurement,
                                      args={'id': graphene.String('Space Heater')})
```
I have tried to create a simple resolver to compute the total_load from water_heater and space_heater values but this total_load resolver always gets called first so both water_heater and space_heater are None, meaning I can't do the calculation.

Both water_heater and space_heater use a DataLoader, part of the get_measurement resolver. This is important as the time series database (InfluxDB) has a tendancy to return quite large amounts of data the I need to resolve from a cache and which need bulk loading.

I have thought about using a promise or middleware to get the total_load but wanted to get a steer as to the right way to resolve this type of timing related issue and more generally how to approach calculated/computed values using both GraphQL and Graphene as much seaching hasn't given me much luck so far.

issue_title: How to use Graphene with Django REST Framework authentication
issue_body:
I got some REST API endpoints in Django and I wanted to use the same authentication for Graphene. The documentation does not provides any guidance.

I have `authentication_classes = (TokenAuthentication,)` in my API views. This was my solution:

urls.py:
```
# ...
from rest_framework.authentication import TokenAuthentication
from rest_framework.permissions import IsAuthenticated
from rest_framework.decorators import authentication_classes, permission_classes, api_view

def graphql_token_view():
    view = GraphQLView.as_view(schema=schema)
    view = permission_classes((IsAuthenticated,))(view)
    view = authentication_classes((TokenAuthentication,))(view)
    view = api_view(['POST'])(view)
    return view

urlpatterns = [
# ...
    url(r'^graphql_token', graphql_token_view()),
    url(r'^graphql', csrf_exempt(GraphQLView.as_view(schema=schema))),
    url(r'^graphiql', include('django_graphiql.urls')),
# ...
```

Note that I added a new `^graphql_token` endpoint and kept the original `^graphql` which is used by the GraphiQL tool.

Then, I set the `Authorization` header in my GraphQL client and point to the `graphql_token` endpoint.

I created this issue in case anyone else has the same question. Also, [posted the question and answer to StackOverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/39026831/how-to-use-graphene-graphql-framework-with-django-rest-framework-authentication/39026832#39026832).

issue_title: Consider "stable" as a stable branch pattern by default
issue_body: If `CONAN_STABLE_BRANCH_PATTERN` environment variable is not set, then package tools treats `master` as the default stable branch pattern.  This is a request to treat both `master` and `stable` as default stable branch patterns.  `stable` is the currently promoted convention, and we'll be publishing over 100 packages using this as the branch name.  It would be cleaner not to have to add this environment variable to each recipe or each CI project, since there's already default logic in place.

issue_title: Add support for linux clang
Module export broken for UPDATE_OPS from AssignSub on a ResourceVariable
issue_body:
## Scope

This issue is filed for the information of *creators* (not consumers) of modules that are trainable and contain UPDATE_OPS such as those used by exponential moving averages or batch normalization. 

This issue is already fixed in TensorFlow at head. Straightforward uses of `tf.layers.batch_normalization()` should be fine already in TensorFlow 1.9rc0.

Regarding concrete modules published on https://tfhub.dev/, a separate issue will be filed in a moment.


## Details

TensorFlow Hub modules created from code like

```python
def module_fn():
  ...
  v = tf.get_variable("v", ...)  # In variable_scope(..., use_resource=True) by default.
  update_op = tf.assign_sub(v, ...)
  tf.add_to_collection(tf.GraphKeys.UPDATE_OPS, update_op)
  ...
```

and used like

```python
m = hub.Module(..., tags={...}, trainable=True)
y = m(x)  # Brings in UPDATE_OPS that depend on x.
with tf.control_dependencies(tf.get_collection(tf.GraphKeys.UPDATE_OPS)):
  train_op = ...
with tf.Session() as session:
  session.run(train_op)
```

did not actually run `update_op` as expected if the module was created with a TensorFlow version before the recent patch https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/commit/bb6532ae227b1f5ce72fbf3bd009f078698324de

The reason is that tf.assign_sub() on a ResourceVariable (the default variable types for Hub modules) does not return a plain Tensor, but a special object of the private type `_UnreadVariable`, which was improperly serialized into the UPDATE_OPS collection of a MetaGraphDef and hence a Hub module. (Non-resource variables are unaffected, but Hub Modules use ResourceVariables by default.)

The same issue applies to `tf.assign()` and `tf.assign_add()`.

`tf.assign_sub()` is used by the TensorFlow utilities to maintain exponential moving averages, including the mean and variance aggregated by batch normalization. Fortunately, `tf.layers.batch_normalization()` in TensorFlow 1.9rc0 ends up calling code in `tf.keras.layers.Layer` that extracts the underlying op from the result of `tf.assign_sub()` before putting it into UPDATE_OPS, which should avoid the issue. However, older or custom implementation of batch normalization may be at risk.


## How to check a module

If you have a model that might be affected, you can inspect its update ops as follows: Take an example use of it like in the code above that instantiates the trainable module and applies it to some example input. Then check that the op types in UPDATE_OPS are the kinds of variable updates that you expect.

```python
_VALID_UPDATE_OPS = {
    "AssignVariableOp", "AssignAddVariableOp", "AssignSubVariableOp",
    # Possibly more, depending on what you do,
    # but *not* "VarHandleOp" or "ReadVariableOp".
}

m = hub.Module(..., tags={...}, trainable=True)
y = m(x)  # Brings in UPDATE_OPS that depend on x.
for item in tf.get_collection(tf.GraphKeys.UPDATE_OPS):
  if isinstance(item, tf.Operation):
    update_op = item
  elif isinstance(item, tf.Tensor):
    update_op = item.op
  else:
    raise TypeError("Item %r of unexpected type" % item)

  if update_op.type not in _VALID_UPDATE_OPS:
    raise ValueError("Bad update op: %r" % update_op)
   
```

issue_title: Commissioning: Add Provisioning URL TLV support
Commissioning: Add Pan ID filter feature to make Joiner join specific Thread network
issue_body:
Currently, Joiner uses broadcast PAN ID(0xffff) as destination Pan ID, and do not filter the received discovery response whether we have configured a specific Pan ID or not. It will finally choose the joiner router who responds the last discovery response (if we have a joiner router on channel 26, it will always choose that one).

Thread Specification 1.1 takes this feature as `MAY`, but it will be very useful especially when we have a test bed to run different tests at the same time.

issue_title: pip installs version 0.9.0 on Python 2.7
issue_body:
Last night I put out the 0.8.6 release which works on either Python 2.7 or 3.4+ and contains various bug fixes.

This morning I put out the 0.9.0 release which only supports Python 3.4+.  I noticed that when using **pip** with a Python 2.7 release that **pip** now attempts to install ``cmd2`` version 0.9.0, which it should not.

We thought we had properly modified **setup.py** so that version 0.9.0 would only attempt to install on Python 3.4+.  Obviously, something is wrong.  We need to either fix **setup.py** or find a way to modify the configuration on PyPI so that on Python 2.7, version 0.8.x installs, but on Python 3.4+ version 0.9.x installs.

issue_title: Support correct error namespace
issue_body: Currently Boulder uses the old 01-draft error namespace (`urn:acme:error:`) but we plan to move to the correct (as of draft-03) namespace `urn:ietf:params:acme:error:` in the near future. In order to easily transition it would probably make sense to support both namespaces for a short period of time.

issue_title: Does the capsule generated on purchasing pyarmor registration bind to a project i.e if anyone replaces the capsule I gave with the capsule available on github, will they be able to use my code then?
issue_body:
When a project is created by Pyarmor (run command init), a project capsule named `.pyarmor_capsule.zip` is generated in project path. It's used to decrypt the source scripts.

In the trial version, all the `.pyarmor_capsule.zip` are same. In the normal version, this capsule is random. No one can run your obfuscated code except there is a `license.lic` generated by pyarmor with this project capsule. The `license.lic` generated by capsule of project A doesn't work in project B.

issue_title: Message upgradability
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Old and new releases of the software must be able to communicate, for this end the off-chain messages format must be forward and backward compatible. 

#### Approaches:

- Every message has a command identifier, when a message is modified a new identifier can be used to differentiate the data
- The serialization format used can support native forward/backward compatibility. By ignoring unknown fields and having default values when possible.
- The Raiden node must ignore messages with an unknown cmd id, and ignore unknown fields for known messages.

#### JSON

JSON can be used in a forward/backward manner if:
- No field without a default is ever renamed/removed. Allowing old versions to understand new messages
- The number of keys is not enforced, allowing new format versions to add fields
- If the above cannot be done, by using a type of message

#### Version handshake

A Raiden node must not assume the node which it's communicating to is running the same version, therefor it must be able to gather information about the capabilities of the partner node. The handshake will be implemented by a new message, which is not known by the first release of the software, to handshake the node's capabilities.

### Task list

- [ ] Add a handshake message
- [ ] Add a state change to change the protocol version of a partner node
- [ ] Add into a state object a way to represent the current version of the node
- [ ] Periodically check the node's capabilities (maybe this should be done in the healtcheck)

issue_title: Simplify ChannelUnlocked filter install
issue_body:
The current implementation of `PaymentChannel.all_events_filter` creates a filter for the event `ChannelUnlocked` based on the participant addresses, this was required because the channel identifier was not available on the batch unlock event.

This can be cleaned up once the channel identifier changes from the smart contracts are integrated, because the new version of the events include the channel identifier.

https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/acf7eec34b4a551b5028d913194ba9da09e4b0e2/raiden/network/proxies/payment_channel.py#L198-L218

issue_title: pld2sobp: adjust for energy
issue_body:
`.pld` files contain the number of MUs (== "meterset") for each spot. This is a number which is proportional to the dose in the Ionization Chamber.

In order to translate this to the amount of particles, one may have to divide with the stopping power to air for that particular energy. 

As a minimum we can extract some number proportional to the number of particles to be delivered in each spot, which is what the MC codes need.

I believe you can see this behavior in the models from tests, such as:

https://github.com/django-polymorphic/django-polymorphic/blob/7fe7861f45ce42c4ced014d8f58c5c5f8603d203/polymorphic/tests/migrations/0001_initial.py#L880-L891


The field kwargs are

```
auto_created=True,
on_delete=django.db.models.deletion.CASCADE,
parent_link=True,
primary_key=True,
serialize=False,
to='tests.Middle'
```

I believe that when the field is created, the `editable` field is not provided, leaving it to default to `True`.

I have observed this in [AWX](https://github.com/ansible/awx) (a Django/DRF app that uses django-polymorphic) models as such:

```python
In [6]: JobTemplate._meta.get_field('unifiedjobtemplate_ptr').editable
Out[6]: True
```

Here, `JobTemplate` is a subclass of the polymorphic base model `UnifiedJobTemplate`, and `JobTemplate.unifiedjobtemplate_ptr` is the automatically-created reference from the subclass table to the base class table.

I think it would be pretty widely agreed that this field should not be editable? This could probably be accomplished by simply adding that kwarg wherever the field is generated.

issue_title: Interrupted `sos execute -q` command leaves falsely submitted jobs
issue_body: I interrupted a `sos execute task_id -q hpc` command which was obviously submitting a job to the PBS queue. The job shows as pending by sos but it never was sent to PBS, and would not be sent to PBS anyway. This is a rare case but we should perhaps improve status detection and/or provide ways to detection or cleanup this case.

issue_title: `Trie.load` raises a DeprecationWarning that doesn't make sense
issue_body:
There's a nonsensical DeprecationWarning in `Trie.load`:

```python
>>> import marisa_trie
>>> t = marisa_trie.Trie()
>>> t.load('data/language_names.marisa')
/home/rspeer/.virtualenvs/lum/bin/ipython:1: DeprecationWarning: Trie.save is deprecated and will be removed in marisa_trie 0.8.0. Please use Trie.load instead.
  #!/home/rspeer/.virtualenvs/lum/bin/python3.5
<marisa_trie.Trie at 0x7f4e9fefb0f0>
```

Some things that are wrong with this:

* I didn't use `Trie.save`, I used `Trie.load`, which is exactly what it's telling me to use.
* It doesn't sound right that `Trie.load` would be able to replace `Trie.save`.
* The warning is actually raised by `Trie.read`, not `Trie.save`.
* `Trie.load` is implemented by *using* `Trie.read`, so there is no way to avoid the DeprecationWarning.

Expected behavior: if I use the function that the DeprecationWarning tells me I should use, I should not get a DeprecationWarning.

issue_title: Fix refresh to work with parallelism
issue_body:
Today's implementation of `refresh` sends source events eagerly to the engine. The engine requires that source events arrive in dependency order. However, the refresh source does not prevent dependent resources from being processed by the engine at the same time, which can result in nondeterministic snapshot validation errors. 

cc @hausdorff @pgavlin

issue_title: Make `--stack` consistent across CLI commands
issue_body:
Today, many commands accept `--stack`, but a few accept a stack via a position argument instead, including at least `pulumi stack rm stackname` and `pulumi stack select stackname`.  For consistency, we should likely make these `pulumi stack rm --stack stackname` and `pulumi stack select --stackname`.

This is tangentially related to https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/1580 which discusses how commands do/should nest under `pulumi stack`, as it's the commands that currently nest under that group which are inconsistent currently.

issue_title: Should we consider removing (and not supporting) recording?
issue_body:
### Description

Recently we've been seeing a lot of people using Streamlink for recording content, and this means they are creating issues related to recording. I am torn on supporting these users as I see very few reasons we should support recording as an application dedicated to streaming (especially the people asking us to support a site or asking for help where they are clearly stealing content). I know that we will still continue to support some forms of recording simply due to how piping can work, but I think we should discuss not supporting these users directly both in question issues, and plugin requests. The number of issues continues to grow with people asking for support or additional plugins and I feel as though the scope of the project is growing in an unsustainable fashion based on our team size. Without additional contributors/maintainers we need to keep a tight focus on what we want to maintain. I don't want to get burnt out but I've definitely been feeling it recently with the constant onslaught of issues.

issue_title: Fail to open a shell
issue_body:
### Bug report

Using `libssh2` examples, I tried opening shell inside a channel in `ssh2-python` and it failed. I need to have a channel and a shell kept open for re-use. I cannot use `exec` because using `exec` to run a command closes the channel (which is expected).

I get a `LIBSSH2_ERROR_SOCKET_DISCONNECT` error code (`-13`) when trying to open the shell.
https://github.com/libssh2/libssh2/blob/master/include/libssh2.h#L451

__Steps to reproduce:__

Run the SSH daemon locally and add an SSH public key to the `authorized_hosts` file of any user (here `root`)

Run the following Python script.

```py
#!/usr/bin/env python
import socket

from ssh2.session import Session
from ssh2.utils import version

sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
sock.connect(("localhost", 22))
session = Session()
session.handshake(sock)
session.userauth_publickey_fromfile(
    "root", "/root/.ssh/id_rsa.pub", "/root/.ssh/id_rsa", ""
)
channel = session.open_session()
print("pty")
err_code = channel.pty()
print(err_code)
print("shell")
err_code = channel.process_startup("shell", "")
print(err_code)

channel.close()
```
```
pty
0
shell
-13
```
__Expected behaviour:__ 0 error code on shell request

__Actual behaviour:__ error on shell request

__Additional info:__ 
libssh2-1:amd64            1.5.0-2ubuntu0.1
libssh2-1-dev:amd64        1.5.0-2ubuntu0.1
python-libssh2             1.0.0-1.2

issue_title: sqli.py test fails on non-3.6 versions of python
issue_body:
So I tried to add a tox.ini with all the versions of py3 + pypy3, due to the results file I realized with 3.6 you get:

```python
2 vulnerabilities found:
Vulnerability 1:
File: example/vulnerable_code/sql/sqli.py
 > User input at line 26, trigger word "get(": 
	param = request.args.get('param', 'not set')
File: example/vulnerable_code/sql/sqli.py
 > reaches line 27, trigger word "execute(": 
	result = db.engine.execute(param)

Vulnerability 2:
File: example/vulnerable_code/sql/sqli.py
 > User input at line 33, trigger word "get(": 
	param = request.args.get('param', 'not set')
File: example/vulnerable_code/sql/sqli.py
 > reaches line 36, trigger word "filter(": 
	result = session.query(User).filter('username={}'.format(param))
```

whereas below 3.6 you get 

```python
2 vulnerabilities found:
Vulnerability 1:
File: example/vulnerable_code/sql/sqli.py
 > User input at line 33, trigger word "get(": 
	param = request.args.get('param', 'not set')
File: example/vulnerable_code/sql/sqli.py
 > reaches line 36, trigger word "filter(": 
	result = session.query(User).filter('username={}'.format(param))

Vulnerability 2:
File: example/vulnerable_code/sql/sqli.py
 > User input at line 26, trigger word "get(": 
	param = request.args.get('param', 'not set')
File: example/vulnerable_code/sql/sqli.py
 > reaches line 27, trigger word "execute(": 
	result = db.engine.execute(param)
```

notice the difference? It's just an order problem

This would explain why I had to change the `results` file to get Travis CI to pass in https://github.com/python-security/pyt/pull/23

issue_title: Monadic behavior
issue_body:
Does it make sense to have an `Outcome[Outcome[T]]`? In some languages/libraries, for example JavaScript `Promise`, this is not possible; instead this is always flattened to `Outcome[T]`.

I don't think such magic is necessary in this library, but thought I'd bring it up.

issue_title: Implement shape inference for Reshape from a Constant shape
issue_body:
in the fully general case, Reshape breaks the flow of shape inference, because the shape may be completely dynamic.

However, many cases are not fully general, and the shape input is the direct output of a Constant operator. In these cases we should be able to inspect the static argument input to the Constant operator, and infer the resulting shape of Reshape output accordingly.

In practice however, this is impossible due to the current shape inference API. There is no access to operators other than that currently being inferred.

This is especially unfortunate because a single Reshape early in the model can block the flow of type information through the entire model.

@linkerzhang @gramalingam @bddppq @dzhulgakov any thoughts on whether/how we might like to fix this? This is the type of thing that would have been trivial if we were building on top of the in-memory graph IR.

Three options I see are

- reintroduce the "shape" (static) argument to Reshape - so that rather than using a Constant node which is inaccessible, the information is available as a direct attribute of the Reshape operator.

- modify the shape inference API to allow other nodes to be visible in some way.

- Add a special case inference implementation for Reshape that bypasses the inference API entirely. It would not be contained in the OpSchema, but would be directly invoked by implementation.h when encountering a Reshape node.
